data.title: Are AI Stocks As Solid As You Think They Are?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

On the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Marta Norton from Empower Investments gets real about what’s driving the AI stock surge and what issues AI investors face. She also covers gold hitting record highs and the Bitcoin buzz that’s got every investor talking.

Mike Larson: Sustainability. That's the word of the day. In other words, can the moves we've seen in everything from AI stocks to gold to Bitcoin sustain themselves? Or are markets increasingly getting out over their skis at the start of the fourth quarter? Of course, investors are contending with many other issues here too, from the government shutdown to an economy that's splintering in housing, in the consumer space, and in the labor markets.

To help unpack what it all means, I'm pleased to have another all new guest on The MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast. She is Marta Norton, Chief Investment Strategist at Empower Investments. Marta, welcome to the program.

Marta, thanks so much for joining me here. First time guest on The MoneyMasters Podcast. I look at this market, I try and come up with an illustration or analogy, and all I can think of is the Energizer Bunny. I mean, it just keeps going and going. So - or what could derail this market, if anything? Or do you think this type of move is justified by the fundamentals and everything you're looking at?

Marta Norton: I would suggest that the move that we've seen thus far, at least the directionality, seems justified. When you take a look at where earnings expectations fell to as Liberation Day unfolded and then how companies have actually performed vis-à-vis those expectations, I think that there's plenty of strong rationale for an upward movement. I think the concern that I would have, and this is something that a lot of folks are articulating, is just the momentum behind it and the degrees, which so much of it hinges on AI and the AI trade and the incestuous deals that are coming out of that space.

So, I think when I'm taking a look at where we go from here, I think there is some downside risk. We have very, very high valuations, so there's low immunity to the stock market. Any outside risk factor could throw it off. And I also think that the health of the economy, the health of the rally, is more concentrated than ideally we would like.

Mike Larson: Got it. And I definitely want to unpack a couple of those points, but first, I just wanted to talk about, I mean, you brought up Liberation Day. To me, it's the resilience of this market. Obviously, a very sharp pullback, the one that we recovered from very quickly. We had geopolitical things floating around in the background. Those don't seem to be derailing the market. What do you think that just the sustainability or the bounce-back appeal that we've seen?

Marta Norton: Well, at first, the bounce back really seemed to be rooted in the fact that the trade embargo against China was taken off the table. Remember, we had those reciprocal tariffs. They go into place, and then immediately, there's this standoff between China and the U.S. emerges. And then that comes off the table, and the market had this strong recovery. But then I think it gained additional ground when we started to see that the tariff effect was not something that would immediately be felt by companies or by consumers.

That doesn't mean and I think here's the mistake that we can fall into that because we haven't seen an immediate effect, it is therefore inconsequential. I think it's still something that's consequential. And one of the things I return to is just the massive expansion we saw in corporate profitability in the wake of globalization. I don't think we'll have that same force behind profitability if we start to shift toward a deglobalization.

And so the idea there being that this is a long drag, not an immediate, running into a wall type of situation. So, to me it seems like, in the immediate future, in the short run, this isn't necessarily a seismic event. In the long run, it's something that we have to contend with.

Mike Larson: Got it. One of the other things I think I have to ask about as we're recording this is the shutdown. It's still out there. We're into week two almost. What are your thoughts on the economic and market impact? Because I know you did one of your updates on LinkedIn the other day, and you said, historically, government shutdowns haven't lasted long. As the economic costs climb, so does political pressure, and you get a self-correcting mechanism.

Marta Norton: Yeah. I do think that there is some self-correction there. Now, there's the outside risk factors that are unique to this particular shutdown. Of course, we are very polarized, parties in the US, there's not a lot of desire to compromise on either front. We also have, I guess, more brinksmanship. So, the Trump administration less likely to just want to come to the table. They also want to introduce some downside risk factors that make Democrats not negotiate.

So, there are some unique elements of this particular shutdown, but I think generally speaking, it's one of those almost a bond market definition. It's not an ability. It's a willingness. And so we have to have that willingness come into play. And I do think as costs escalate, you're going to see parties negotiate this interest to come out of the shadows. One of the things that I think is especially interesting is the lack of official government data at a moment where we're very--.

And so, I think there's, just risk that we extrapolate our own views about data to offset those views. I don't think that's necessarily something that is going to change the course of the Fed, but it's something that we have to be watching as we're trying to interpret private market data or trying to ascertain what would government data be if we had it. I think it just creates additional uncertainty at a moment where we're really looking for clarity.

Mike Larson: Got it. And let's pivot to the Fed here. I mean, we've got a couple more meetings in 2025 and, obviously, a few after then in the first quarter of 2026. What are your thoughts on how things unfold?

Marta Norton: Well, I think in 2025, at least, there's decent alignment between the market and the Fed. So, in expectation for two additional cuts, I think when we talk about a lack of data, maybe additional uncertainty, I think that maybe tilts the margin in favor of an additional cut just in favor of that risk management approach that Powell has talked about.

I think the bigger disconnect between the market and the Fed occurs over the course of 2026. One thing that's been super fascinating to watch repeatedly throughout the past several years has been how much the market has gotten ahead of the Federal Reserve. The market gets so excited about cuts and then has to fall back because the Fed's not quite there yet. And I think there's a little bit of that when we take a look at what the Federal Reserve estimate as that long-term Fed funds rate, it's around 3%. And the market's saying that we'll get there by this time next year.

And that seems to be either the result of inflation that perfectly and quickly deflates or the result of a labor market that's weaker than the equity market actually thinks that it is. So, it strikes me that that 3% expectation over the course of the year is either too optimistic or not rooted in the right argument, given how strong the equity market’s been.

Mike Larson: Got it. And when it comes to the underlying economy, you had a piece, your Q4 outlook calling the market, excuse me, the economy splintering, whether housing, consumer spending, and so on. Can you elaborate on what that point means?

Marta Norton: Yeah. So, when we take a look at the headline numbers, whether we're looking at the labor market and/or we're looking at GDP or any other metric on how the economy is doing, those numbers are reasonable. Earnings are strong. There's a lot of reasons to think that we're in a healthy place, but when you think about what the individual is experiencing, that's really, I use the word splintering because it really varies based on a number of different criteria.

Are you low income or high income? Do you own a house, or do you not own a house? Do you have a big investment portfolio, or do you not have a big investment portfolio? Are you early in your career, entry level, in which case the unemployment rate is a bit higher, or are you later in your career and more established? If you cut along these different dimensions, you might have a very different view on how healthy the economy is. And the interesting thing about this, and I'm sure this is always true to a certain degree. Right? Each person's experience is a little bit different, but it seems more extreme in the current environment. And I think it creates a policy dilemma because it's hard to manage two different splintering groups. Right?

And I think it also has maybe more political ramifications because it depends on how different people are interpreting the economy based on how they vote and like. So, I think it's something that we're really wrestling with as we start to take a look at 2026 and get a sense for what are the ramifications of it.

Mike Larson: Got it. If we look at markets here, I mean, you brought it up earlier, the AI trade, the dominance of being in the market in general. And now there's questions being raised about the circular nature of some of these deals and so on. What are the risks or opportunities there? What are your thoughts on that trend?

Marta Norton: It's so tricky, and I think this is something that a lot of us are wrestling with. The AI train seems real. Right? This seems to be a truly innovative technology that can transform the economy, whether you're thinking about something like healthcare and how research and development is done, or you're thinking about something as -- as call centers and how you can automate that.

I mean, there's so many different applications that could really improve earnings and improve revenue. But the question is, how much of this is priced in, and what timing are we looking at? Are we expecting a straight line higher, which is how it has felt since April 8, just the straight line higher where AI adoption is just perfectly implemented over time, or are we looking at something that's more fits and starts?

And I guess, I think from a realistic point of view, fits and starts makes more sense. And so, I think we can and should expect more DeepSeek moment as AI unfolds. And DeepSeek was interesting because that focused on the supply question, but I think a more troubling question for AI would be the demand side.

Is this actually as profound as we think it is? And so, I think as we have those moments of doubt, to me, those are the better investment moments than chasing the euphoria that seems to be dominant in the market today.

Mike Larson: Got it. I guess the other big trend that I'm seeing is the thing we're dubbing at the debasement trade, whatever term you want to call it. I mean, we've got gold the day we're recording, futures besting $4,000. Bitcoin topped 125,000 over the weekend again. That's a sustainable move or what are your thoughts on that trend that we're seeing?

Marta Norton: Well, I mean, it depends on how we're measuring sustainability. Do I think it's going to necessarily collapse in the immediate future? No. But I think one of the things we have to determine as, each of us on our own, is, how we make determinations on when something is a Buy and when something is a Sell. And the struggle that I have with something like gold or Bitcoin is determining what that fair value is. Because for a lot of investments, what you want to do is either buy something at or below its fair value and then see that appreciation. But with gold, with Bitcoin, there aren't really cash flows that you can identify that say, this is what this investment is worth. This is how it generates wealth.

And so, I think what we ultimately end up doing is chasing the technicals. Other people are buying, so we start to buy. And that's fine, and it can last for a really long time. That doesn't mean it shouldn't be in your portfolio, but I think you should recognize that there might not be as strong a foundation to that investment as you think. And so, when it sells off, how do you respond? And so that's one of the things that I think about with those investments.

I don't think there's anything wrong holding them in a portfolio, but I do think people want to understand why they're moving higher and what they would do, what their recourse is going to be if they start to sell off.

Mike Larson: Got it. We are in the equity markets. I mean, we've talked about tech. Are there other trends you're watching that you find interesting and opportunistic? I mean, we've seen, for example, another big banking merger. I'm wondering maybe we have some on financials.

Marta Norton: Well, I mean, financials have been a pretty good place to be really in the wake of the regional banking crisis that we had several years ago. You had this massive valuation opportunity open up. It was a great time to invest. In fact, I looked at research that shows that in the wake of a crisis, that's the great time to move into financials. The ones that stick around tend to do quite well and outpace the S&P 500. And I think there are some overarching, I guess, catalysts in the space, whether we're looking at deregulation, for example, within financials or more broadly. I think deregulation is a big theme. I think the CapEx cycle mostly related to AI, but also broadly when you think of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, a 100% expensing. I think that's another big theme to rely on, but what I keep coming back to, and I know I beat a tired drum here, but I keep coming back to what's in the price. And I think a lot of this good news is priced in not just for tech, but it's priced in in industrial. It's priced in in consumer staples. It's priced in in financials. And so, there are some real themes out there. I just don't know what's not in the price at this point.

Mike Larson: Yeah. Well, one of the reasons we're speaking is, obviously, we're honored to have you speaking at our MoneyShow Masters Symposium in Sarasota, which is at the beginning of December. There’s still some time between now and then, but what are your thoughts on maybe some messages, a few bullet points you might share with attendees there, and your early thoughts about how you think we're going to be when we roll into 2026?

Marta Norton: Well, I'm super excited to attend. It's great timing. It's December, and Chicago in December is not the best. So, I'll be thrilled to be in Florida. And we'll be just finishing up our 2026 outlook at that point. So, I'll be sharing what is our outlook for the economy, for stocks, for bonds. And we're focusing in on three key research areas.

I think the housing market is super fascinating at this point, the wealth that's been created in the housing market, how we can have affordability more broadly. That's one topic that we're going to dig into. I also think the sovereign debt question is really important both in the U.S. and outside the U.S., and thinking through what does that mean for the yield curve, particularly at the longer end, and what our expectations should be.

Of course, we can't avoid AI. So, we're going to continue to explore that topic and especially around questions of how is AI demand manifesting itself and what can we expect going forward?

Mike Larson: Well, Marta, listen, I want to thank you so much for joining me here. Appreciate you taking some time out to share your thoughts. Viewers, if you're watching this, be sure to join us at the MoneyShow Masters Symposium, it's in Sarasota. It's an all-new venue, the Ritz-Carlton, Downtown. That's December 1st to 3rd, and you can find a link in the description below. Gives you complete details, lets you know what you need to do if you're interested in joining us. Again, Marta, looking forward to seeing you there, and great talking to you today.

Marta Norton: Yes. My pleasure. Thank you.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: American International Group: A Mega Insurer To Keep In A Dividend Portfolio
data.description:
The Stock: A NYC-Based Insurer Seeing Market Momentum Lately

October reminds me of the crisp autumn days in New York City, so in this fall spirit today's article follows up on a NYC-based mega insurer, American International Group (NYSE:AIG).

Since my buy rating last April, the stock is up +3.1%, and recent momentum data shows my bullish sentiment justified as AIG outperformed the S&P 500 in the last month:

Some key upside drivers for me in the last rating were new business wins as a growth driver, strong market share in its space, and the productivity tailwind in this sector driven by AI.

Don't take just my word for it, though, since just a few weeks ago this stock was mentioned in Yahoo! Finance who cited Zack's Investment Research featuring this stock and saying that "so far this year, shares have seen a price change of 5.36%."

As a heads up, for readers rather new to this stock or my articles, the business profile of AIG says it "offers insurance products for commercial, institutional, and individual customers in North America and internationally."

Today's article follows up on AIG since its early-August earnings call, and the goal of my updated research this time was to see if my prior rating would change.

The Thesis: Time To Pull Back Into Neutral Gear on AIG

Based on my updated research today, I am lowering my prior buy rating to a hold, and here is the worksheet I made supporting that score:

While the key upside drivers for AIG are macro-level demand as well as fair valuation, impressive EBITDA margins, and some attractive balance sheet metrics, it is impacted by low potential upside to its share price along with weakened top-line growth factors and impacts from catastrophe loss events. As a dividend idea, it has proven itself as a dividend grower.

The Sector: Commercial Insurance Could See A Macro Tailwind, But Also An Impact From Rates

To talk about this sector, let's first consider what niche of insurance AIG is in. Its key areas seem to be property and casualty insurance for businesses, among other solutions, and also some non-life insurance solutions for individuals and families. Remember, for instance, that AIG spun off its life and retirement insurance solutions business, Corebridge Financial (CRBG), some years ago into a separate company.

So, I think macro-level forecasts indicating demand for property/casualty insurance has been worth considering.

Consider, for instance, that this past June it was reported in Yahoo! Finance, citing data from Austin Texas-based market analysis firm SNS Insider, that:

The Commercial Insurance Market is valued at USD 869.37 billion in 2023 and is expected to reach USD 1660.29 billion by 2032, growing at a CAGR of 7.5% in the forecast period from 2024 to 2032.

In fact, the AIG brand was mentioned in the forecast as a major player in this space and part of that tailwind:

Such forecasts, if they prove true, obviously could drive upside for this sector and its key names which include AIG, the subject of my article today.

While there may be some forecasts pointing to macro demand in this space, we should also consider other data showing that insurance rates have come down recently and I believe that could be a negative downside in the short-term.

For instance, Marsh wrote in its 2025 Global Insurance Market Index that

Global commercial insurance rates declined by 4% in the second quarter of 2025, the fourth consecutive decrease in the composite rate following seven years of increases. A continuing increase in insurer competition was the main catalyst behind rate trends, which declined globally in every region except the US, where rates remained flat.

With both new and established insurers vigorously competing for business, we expect the overall trends to continue throughout 2025, barring unforeseen changes in conditions.

So, this type of outlook by Marsh could be considered a downside due to the negative impact of lower rates.

In the property/casualty insurance market, Seeking Alpha lists several competitors/peers so I picked these 3: The Hartford (HIG), Chubb (CB), and Allstate (ALL). Within this sector peer group, all peers have a predominantly optimistic rating across the board it seems, giving further confidence to this sector, despite the rate headwinds:

Based on the above points presented, my impression in this category of sector/industry is moderately bullish.

Revenue Growth: Q2 Saw Almost Flat Premium Growth, While A Key Peer Is Worth Watching

Now, looking past the overall sector, I took another look at company-specific growth drivers.

Although the Q2 results are already historic data by now, as they came out in early August and now it's October, I think we can pull some relevant points there to get some idea of top-line growth impact.

While there did not seem to be specific mention of organic growth of new policyholders, in the Q2 results presentation there was a lot of talk about YoY comparisons in net premiums written/NPW. According to this graphic below, if you compare 2Q24 vs 2Q25 as reported, there was a very slight YoY decline in NPW across their 3 key insurance lines, so practically flat growth attained:

Of the 3 segments shown, it seems the North America Commercial segment saw the best YoY growth in net premiums, which is a key metric followed in the insurance business.

Considering that reinsurance could impact net written premiums, in searching for what could have impacted these numbers according to Q2 commentary(pg 2) the Global Personal Insurance segment had a "six-point negative impact to Global Personal’s topline growth," driven by "changes to reinsurance structures in our High Net Worth business."

Looking ahead, CEO Peter Zaffino had an optimistic view for the rest of the year, saying in the Q2 comments that "we have tremendous momentum heading into the second half of 2025 and remain very confident in our ability to achieve our long-term financial targets."

While that may add some confidence to some analysts and investors, let's also consider what Seeking Alpha data shows and it is that when compared to these peers I selected in the property/casualty space it seems AIG not only has the weakest historic revenue growth but also the worst expected forward revenue growth:

For instance, I think the closest comparable would be Chubb due to its size and exposure to commercial lines, and it is racing ahead in the revenue category.

Given that point, and the others made above, I am leaning in the neutral camp when it comes to this category.

Profit margins: Among Leaders In Peer Group For Operating Margin, But Prone To Loss Events

Let's move on profit margins. The fact is, after having written over 200+ articles on this platform, I will not claim there is a perfect science to earnings growth but rather I prefer to consider key profit margins in comparison to similar peers, to get a sense of whether my target company is a major outlier from its peer group or in line with it.

With that said, I put greater weight on the EBITDA margin because it excludes non-operating expenses like depreciation, and in this case it is nearly +30% vs the sector avg. of around 22%, which is impressive.

The net income margin, which could be impacted by non-operating expenses which lower reported profits, was much lower at 11.6%, and lower than the sector average of 23%.

Since I put greater weight on the EBITDA, I think an upside here is that AIG has proven itself as an efficient profit generator from its core business activities, and continuation of that trend could positively impact future earnings growth.

If we were to look at another angle, this time the operating margin, we can see in my peer comparison chart below that AIG with an operating margin of 17.8%, is on the higher end of this peer group, but also that the trend line shows an improvement trend over the last year:

Both of these margin examples above are indicators there is upside potential in this stock and its ability to drive earnings when compared to similar (though not necessarily the same size) insurers.

A key expense driver in this type of business is having to pay out policy benefits, and that is the nature of this industry, with income statement data showing AIG paid out $3.4B in benefits in the quarter ending June.

A future potential downside could come from major catastrophe losses, since we have seen that reality play out during the California wildfires last January, impacting profits. For instance, The Financial Times reported last February that "AIG expected losses of about $500mn from the firestorms, which destroyed more than 16,000 homes and businesses in January."

Because some of these events are impossible to predict with 100% very far in advance, I think it leaves insurers with the choice of either having to hike up rates in a certain area known for catastrophic loss events, or pull out of that region all together. The same Financial Times article pointed out, for instance, that it was 2022 "when the company stopped offering new policies to most homeowners in California to focus on businesses and the richest residents of the state," despite this move not completely preventing the hit they took from the fires, it seems.

So, while I am bullish on this firm's margins, I also am cautious about being overly confident about stable future earnings growth due to the above-mentioned scenario, and so I am on the low bullish end here.

Cashflow Trends: Despite A Recent Hit To Operating Cash, Longer-Term Dividend Growth Is Proven

As this is a dividend-paying stock, we should touch upon cashflow trends since they can support dividend stability and future growth.

Seeking Alpha shows that AIG has had a negative YoY operating cashflow growth of -52%, even though the sector avg. nearly reached 12%. The forward operating cashflow growth is barely 3%.

We can see from cashflow data that the quarter ending March saw a negative impact to operating cashflow, and incidentally that is the quarter with the January wildfire event in California which I previously said had a huge impact on AIG:

Because this is a one-quarter impact and does not seem to be indicative of a longer trend of cashflow decline, it should be viewed in that context.

Also, despite that difficult quarter, AIG still has behind it a proven track record of continually keeping dividends stable and growing them too, with a 10 year CAGR of 10%.

So, what I think we can expect from a business like this is occasional future impacts to operating cash if there is a major catastrophe event impacting its policyholders and their property, but also that AIG has proven to be steadfast over a longer period and didn't even seem to cut dividends during the 2020 pandemic but continued to have the cashflow to support them.

That adds to the case that the cashflow story deserves at least a hold/neutral rating.

Balance Sheet: An Upgrade From S&P, Low D/E, And Highly Liquid Investment Portfolio

We've come to the part where I focus on a few points related to balance sheet risk, and today we'll look at some credit ratings and also the debt/equity ratio.

Notable to mention is that last May, major agency S&P Global (SPGI) upgraded this firm. In their rating note, they said:

S&P Global Ratings raised its financial strength rating and issuer credit ratings on AIG core subsidiaries to ‘AA-‘ from ‘A+’ and revised the core subsidiaries outlook to stable from positive.

In addition to derisking the portfolio and bolstering its reserves, AIG also made significant progress deleveraging its balance sheet.

I think that is great news and should've gotten the attention of many analysts and investors by now, another reason I think there has been momentum in the last 6 months in the share price, as I said at the start.

As for the debt-to-equity ratio, here is how AIG compares to its peers I selected:

From the chart above, with a D/E of just 0.22, AIG has not only the lowest debt/equity of this peer group but also its trend line shows it has gradually come down since 2023, a great sign, and also correlates with what S&P Global was saying about the de-leveraging trend.

Given these points above, it seems the liability side of the balance sheet looks appealing.

On the asset side, it is worth mentioning that AIG holds a significant portfolio of investments it earns income on:

From the chart above (from Q2 presentation, pg 11), what sticks out is that the majority of its portfolio exposure is to fixed income securities that it can trade, and so I think this provides a high level of liquidity since such assets can generally be sold in the market.

However, sometimes they have to be sold at a loss, if their fair value has dropped. In fact, income statement data shows that AIG has sold investments at a loss for all of the last 5 quarters.

Experienced readers of mine already would know that rising interest rates lead to declining bond values (inverse relationship between rates and bond values) and so the question then is will we see a prolonged period of lower rates and higher bond values soon, adding to the upside for this balance sheet.

Given the recent Fed rate cut, we may be seeing a reversal of the high interest rate period in recent years, so that is a good sign, I think.

With that said, I am in the cautious buy range on this category.

Valuation: Fairly Valued Opportunity Tracking Its Sector and Moving Average

We will briefly discuss valuation and whether I think there is an opportunity there.

Valuation data shows a fwd P/E (GAAP) of 12.60, which is very close to the sector average so we can say it is almost in line with it.

At the same time, on the 5-year price chart I made below, AIG is trading slightly above its 200-day simple moving average:

So, it is neither too overvalued vs its sector nor is it trading too far above or below its average. I think both of these are justifiable and perhaps even slightly over-cautious since there have been 12 upward EPS revisions to this stock, and consensus EPS estimates expecting 20-30% YoY growth in the next two years.

Because this appears to be fairly valued, I am giving it a buy score in this category, which I give to stocks who are either fairly valued or an undervaluation opportunity.

Future Price Target: A Mere 1% Upside Expected

To determine a future price target for this stock and potential upside to the price, I created this worksheet below:

From this worksheet, a mere +1% further upside is expected vs Wednesday afternoon's share price. This barely justifies a hold rating so that is what is getting here, and unfortunately this lowers the stock's overall score today.

For those curious enough, the way I got to this target was: my own price target of $81.14 is based on fwd P/E x consensus EPS estimate. That target is then averaged with the Wall St avg consensus target of $88.22, to get an avg price target for this stock of $84.68.

Challenge To My Rating: More Favorable Fed Policy Leading To Higher Bond Values

Since I called this stock a hold this time, which is a neutral rating that could also be interpreted as cautiously optimistic since it is not a sell, a factor that could impact this rating and cause it to be overly cautious is that if the Fed continues cutting its target interest rates and over the next few years it leads to all other rates falling it could boost the value of this firm's bond portfolio, boosting its book value and this is an upside factor.

The latest from rate tracker CME Fedwatch shows at least a 92% probability of another rate cut at the Fed's October meeting, so I am keeping an eye on this space as it could impact insurers and banks holding a considerable amount of fixed-income securities.

In the reader comments, I welcome your views on this topic and how you see the role of Fed decisions impacting a fixed-income portfolio like that of AIG?

Conclusion: A Great Stock To Hold Rather Than Buy Now

While I have generally loved the insurance sector in many of my earlier articles, today on AIG I am compelled to be in a holding pattern this time around and I have shown why.

In conclusion, my impression of AIG now is that of a highly necessary business as the global demand for P&C insurance is continuous, but also has to face challenges like competitors, lower rates, and catastrophe events.

Its subsidiaries have gotten an impressive upgrade from S&P, and it has been de-leveraging while also growing dividends, so that along with some nice EBITDA margins seem to push the needle forward but there seems to be limited upside expected in the share price and that is unfortunately holding back this stock.

data.title: Rubrik: Incredible Accelerating Growth
data.description:
The stock market continues to hover around all-time highs, and I continue to emphasize rotating out of large-cap tech stocks and into faster-growing but less visible small- and mid-cap names. In my view, this relatively underappreciated market segment will be the best bulwark against potential market downside.

Rubrik, Inc. (NYSE:RBRK) presents an especially compelling buying opportunity. The data resilience software company is showcasing industry-leading growth rates north of >50% y/y. It is true that shares of Rubrik are up nearly ~30% year to date, but the stock has corrected meaningfully from summer highs around $100, presenting a fresh buying opportunity in this attractive name.

I last wrote a buy article on Rubrik in June, when the stock was trading in the low $90s. Since then, Rubrik has achieved a fantastic beat-and-raise quarter, which also showcased revenue growth accelerating north of >50% y/y. At the same time, the stock has also fallen, taking some sting out of the stock's premium valuation multiples. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

First, let's discuss Rubrik's substantial guidance increase and the stock's valuation against the latest estimates.

As shown in the chart above, Rubrik has boosted its full-year revenue to $1.227-$1.237 billion, which represents a fantastic 38%-40% y/y growth rate, a substantial raise versus a previous 32%-34% y/y expectation. Also, given the fact that revenue is currently on an accelerating trend and 1H'25 growth has clocked in at 50% y/y, I'd say there's still quite a bit of room for Rubrik to beat-and-raise again throughout the remainder of the year. Meanwhile, for FY27 (the year for Rubrik ending in January 2027), Wall Street analysts are expecting the company to see more moderate 25% y/y growth, to $1.54 billion in revenue.

At current share prices near $85, Rubrik trades at a market cap of $16.99 billion. After we net off the $1.53 billion of cash and $1.13 billion of convertible debt on the company's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $16.59 billion.

This positions Rubrik's valuation multiples at:

  • 13.5x EV/FY26 revenue.
  • 10.8x EV/FY27 revenue.

To me, it would be quite a big stretch to call Rubrik a value stock. I'd say these are quite compelling valuation multiples when other software companies like JFrog Ltd. (FROG) and ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) trade at similar low-teens multiples, despite commanding only ~20% y/y growth.

To me, Rubrik is still in the early stages of capturing its large $53 billion global TAM. As a reminder for investors who are newer to this name, here is what I consider to be the core bull case catalysts for the company:

  • Unique product within cybersecurity (resilience) that distinguishes it from its peers. While many other high-profile cybersecurity companies focus on attack prevention (such as Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) and Zscaler, Inc. (ZS)), Rubrik's focus on resilience (how to respond and get systems back online after an attack) positions the company as a category leader in an area of cybersecurity with fewer competitors.
  • Consolidation in cybersecurity. There are big-ticket deals happening in the cybersecurity space, including Palo Alto Networks' recent $25 billion purchase of CyberArk. Though not in itself a reason to buy Rubrik, the possibility of M&A for an attractive, high-growth asset like Rubrik provides additional support for its share price.
  • AI is proving to be a growth accelerator for the company. For many tech stocks, "AI" is just a buzzword while recent product releases have done little to actually provide incremental growth. This isn't the case for Rubrik, which has seen its growth rates climb amid new AI releases.
  • Economies of scale. Rubrik is tipping quickly toward breakeven, thanks to its strong 80%+ pro forma gross margins that allow for substantial operating leverage at scale.

Stay long here: Rubrik is well poised to reclaim prior highs.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Rubrik's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Rubrik's revenue climbed 51% y/y to $309.9 million, smashing past Wall Street's expectations of $282.3 million (+38% y/y) by a massive 13-point margin. We note as well that revenue growth improved 2 points sequentially versus 49% y/y growth in Q1, which is impressive on two counts. First, growth that is above 40% is already quite rare in the software industry and very difficult to accelerate from; second, Rubrik is also facing a tough macroeconomy that has seen many enterprise software peers report a slowdown in deal closings.

And as a reminder to investors, the majority of Rubrik's revenue is recurring and subscription-based. Subscription revenue grew at an even faster 55% y/y pace to $297.0 million. The company's ARR also grew 36% y/y to $1.25 billion, adding $71 million in net-new ARR in the quarter.

Another metric on which Rubrik scores quite well is net revenue retention rates, which clocked in "above 120%" in Q2. As a reminder, NRR measures revenue growth from existing customers in the install base. Again, this distinguishes Rubrik from other software companies, many of whom have struggled to even maintain ~110% net revenue retention rates in the current climate. For the industry, this has been a function of declining seat counts: since most software companies charge per head, layoffs and a slowdown in hiring have hurt expansion. Rubrik, however, prices based on consumption and data volumes, which have been buoyed in the AI era.

Speaking to how Gen AI has driven demand for Rubrik solutions, CEO Bipul Sinha noted as follows on the Q2 earnings call:

Simply meaning they are certain that cyber attacks are inevitable despite the significant investments they have made in cyber prevention and detection. At the same time, these enterprises are also looking to replatform and modernize their infrastructure in preparation for the imminent enterprise AI transformation. As companies shift deeper into cloud and GenAI, customers continue to turn to us, Rubrik, for complete cyber resilience, delivering uniform and consistent data security policy control as well as rapid, accurate recovery from cyberattacks. Concurrently, our Predibase acquisition, which I'll discuss later in my remarks, also allows us to deliver enterprise AI acceleration.

The bottom line is this, we have tremendous opportunities ahead of us. First, we continue to lead the vast cyber resilience market. And second, at the same time, we continue to build a new future for enterprise AI. Now, I'll detail some of the wins across our initiatives at varying scales. For our cyber-resilient data protection business, we continue to add solutions across new applications and workloads, leveraging the same underlying preemptive recovery engine to deliver risk and remediation capabilities.

On the profitability front, we note that Rubrik achieved a 5-point lift in pro forma gross margins to 82%, which the company attributed to both revenue outperformance, headcount efficiency in the customer support org, as well as new cloud contracts that led to lower hosting costs.

Additional leverage, particularly on Rubrik's largest expense line item (sales and marketing), helped drive pro forma operating losses to a near-breakeven -$4.4 million, with losses shrinking 93% y/y. Pro forma operating margin of -1.4% improved more than 30 points y/y, versus -29.7% in the year-ago Q2.

Key takeaways

We note that thanks to its fierce growth, Rubrik is classified as a "Rule of 40" company, even though the company is just on the tip of achieving pro forma profitability. In my view, this is a strong and enduring asset to own: Rubrik's ~50% y/y growth illustrates the very greenfield nature of its cyber resilience market. Stay long here and hold out for a broad rebound.

data.title: Anbio Biotechnology's Hype And Hard Approvals Suggest A Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Anbio Biotechnology (NASDAQ:NNNN) is a medical instruments company that develops in-vitro (IVD) test devices. Its solutions can be useful for over-the-counter (OTC), point-of-care (POC), and lab applications. Additionally, NNNN's EU sales depend on upgrading legacy IVDD marks to IVDR, which requires securing a Notified Body and clearing a higher clinical-evidence bar. As for the US, NNNN's commercialization efforts still await approval and are payor-dependent. It's also worth mentioning that NNNN's governance remains a risk, as approximately 99% of voting power is in the Class B shares. Plus, it's incredibly challenging to justify their current valuation even under optimistic growth assumptions. So, I have to remain bearish on the stock at these levels.

IVD Medical Devices

Anbio Biotechnology is a medical device firm focused on in vitro diagnostics (IVD). NNNN produces tests and instruments for over-the-counter (OTC) and laboratory use. The company was incorporated in 2021 as a private Cayman Islands firm, and it had its IPO on the Nasdaq in February 2025. NNNN has executive offices in Frankfurt am Main, Germany. I previously covered NNNN last June, and since then, the stock has massively appreciated by around 254%. However, a lot of the concerns I had back then still remain, so I thought it would be worthwhile to write an update.

As a quick recap, NNNN's portfolio presents a broad set of biomarkers for detecting a wide range of conditions. This includes infectious diseases, oncology conditions, cardiovascular and endocrine disorders, inflammation, renal disease, diabetes, pharmacogenomics (genetics that affect a person's response to medicines), and drug-of-abuse testing. NNNN's strategy centers on affordable and portable testing platforms to allow diagnostics closer to patients for OTC and point-of-care testing (POCT). However, going forward, its growth will depend on successfully scaling up its non-COVID assays (more on this later).

Furthermore, NNNN's product lines include a fluorescent immunoassay (FIA) platform that uses antibodies plus a fluorescent label to detect and quantify targets. Such targets could include antigens or a particular antibody in a patient sample. NNNN's FIA then uses a small reader and analyzer to measure fluorescence to obtain an accurate numeric result. Interestingly, this platform can be applied to detect infectious diseases like influenza A/B, COVID-19, RSV, and dengue, among others. Also, it could be helpful for evaluating cardiac, endocrine, fertility, and inflammatory conditions.

Another product line comprises a dry chemistry platform, with a portable analyzer and a menu for chemistry panels to be used at POCT and for laboratory use. This technology uses dry reagents like slides, strips, and discs instead of liquid reagents. The patient sample is applied to a disposable element, where the reaction occurs, and the device reads the results. This platform is used to evaluate metabolic and organ function with measurement of glucose, urea, creatinine, uric acid, liver panel, lipids, electrolytes, and other options. The dry chemistry platform is used in POC settings, small & medium labs, physician offices, veterinary labs, and in general in resource-limited sites that benefit from room-temperature reagents.

Additionally, NNNN's chemiluminescent immunoassay (ChLIA) solution is a platform that uses antibodies tagged with an enzyme or label that produces light during a chemical reaction. The analyzer measures that light and converts the intensity into a quantitative result for the target to classify between an antigen and an antibody. This solution is used to test endocrine function, hormones, cardiac markers, infectious disease serology, oncology and tumor markers, and inflammation (CRP, PCT). ChLIA offers higher analytical sensitivity and tighter precision than FIA, but FIA wins on portability. ChLIA is ideal for core and midsize labs and near-patient POCT, so at the end of the day, its competitiveness depends on the final use case.

Concentrated Control On Class B Shares

Since June, NNNN's share price has been incredibly volatile. In my view, the stock price is prone to such outsized moves because only a small slice of the total outstanding shares actually trade. Remember, there are only 1.6 million shares from the IPO, but the 20-F disclosed a weighted average shares outstanding of 42.3 million. Thus, when supply is thin, even small buy/sell orders can quickly push the stock price.

Also, NNNN has a dual-class setup that places around 99% of voting power with two Class B holders. These are CVC Investment and Northwestern Investment, and their Class B shares carry 50 votes each but no economic rights (no dividends or residual value). And effectively, this does concentrate control within those two institutions, which I believe should actually translate into a discount rather than a valuation premium. And when you couple that with a small public float, I think it helps explain why the stock has been so volatile since its IPO.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, NNNN currently trades at a $1.4 billion market cap, which is remarkably pricey given its fundamentals. Since NNNN is an FPI, that means we only get annual updates akin to a 10-K report from a regular US issuer. The latest 20-F from NNNN is now a bit outdated, as it was filed back in April 2025. However, we know that in December 2024, NNNN's balance sheet had $11.8 million in cash against no financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities). Its book value reached $17.2 million, which would now imply an extremely expensive P/B of 81.4.

Having said that, NNNN's IPO gave us a thin float of 1.6 million shares (Class A) and raised around $8.0 million in gross proceeds. So, if we add that to its book value, we could estimate an "updated" figure at around $25.2 million. However, that would hint at a pricey P/B of 55.6, which is still much higher compared to its peers' median P/B of just 2.9. Thus, I imagine the premium is largely tied to its PCR systems and newly announced Chikungunya rapid test. Their PCR products are indeed interesting, and the EuroMedLab, AVA, and ACMTT exhibitions announced a promising chemistry lineup for testing.

However, I believe the excitement is mostly around their potential with AP-100 Ultra-Fast PCR and Chikungunya. The story is simple: NNNN can soon offer a handheld PCR test that takes 15 minutes and gives results comparable to laboratories. In theory, this could definitely capture a sizeable market, though even that is difficult to quantify. Plus, Chikungunya outbreaks can definitely benefit from faster testing solutions. However, even at its COVID-19 heights, NNNN's revenues only reached $23.5 million in 2022. And I find it incredibly difficult to reach those sales again with Chikungunya alone, even if its outbreaks escalate in the coming years. Plus, their PCR testing announcements didn't include FDA approvals as far as I could find.

Therefore, for the most part, NNNN's products are CE-marked under the old IVDD, which allowed them to be CE-certified by manufacturer self-declaration. However, looking ahead, NNNN's ability to commercialize its products in the EU will require an upgrade to IVDR. And securing an updated IVDR will need a Notified Body (NB) review and a CE certificate. Basically, the EU intends to increase the bar in terms of clinical evidence and post-market surveillance. And NNNN stated in its 20-F form that it hasn't yet formalized an NB partnership and is updating technical files. In practice, this does create some timing risk given industry-wide NB involvement, and the IVDR transition deadlines go from 2027 to 2029.

Likewise, NNNN's US situation with its products remains in "pre-clearance." This means that its OTC use must have an FDA authorization to be widely commercialized. Until then, a physician's office can use NNNN's tests only if it holds a valid CLIA waiver. On top of that, NNNN's product adoption is largely payor-dependent. This is because using its testing products doesn't have automatic insurance coverage and reimbursement, so each payer decides whether or not it covers NNNN's tests on a case-by-case basis.

Lastly, if we include the $8.0 million raised from their IPO, I estimate NNNN's cash reached $19.8 million. And its 20-F disclosed NNNN generated $2.1 million in cash flows from operations. So, from that perspective, I would say NNNN seems relatively self-sustainable as long as they don't massively ramp up their CAPEX or R&D expenses going forward. But, even then, I would argue that's unlikely given their ambitions to scale manufacturing of their new Ultra-Fast PCR tests and meet regulatory requirements. Yet, until we get an update, we should assume they have enough financing for the near term.

Conclusion: Still Bearish

Overall, I have to say I remain skeptical about NNNN's prospects. Even if the bull case materializes, I doubt it will be enough to justify its already expensive price tag. That caps its upside potential even in the blue-sky scenario. And on the other hand, if NNNN fails to execute or faces regulatory setbacks, it could quickly translate into substantial shareholder losses. This is particularly true since its stock has a very thin float and trading volume. Thus, I believe it's fair to reiterate my "Sell" rating on NNNN at these levels.
